name: General OPD visit
documents: NHIS card, hospital folder or card (if returning), referral letter if you were referred, valid ID if asked.
bring: Comfortable clothing, water bottle, small snack for long waits, your own pen for forms, cash or mobile money for items not covered.
process: Register or renew folder at records / front desk.
Pay any required levies or show NHIS as directed.
Vitals (BP, temperature, weight) may be taken at triage or nurses’ station.
Wait for your queue number to be called for consultation.
See the doctor; you may be sent for lab, pharmacy, or imaging.
Complete pharmacy or lab steps before leaving if prescribed.
nhis: Consultation often covered on active NHIS; some drugs, consumables, or certain tests may be partly or fully out-of-pocket depending on facility policy.
time: About 2–4 hours total on busy days; longer if labs or imaging are needed.
tips: Arrive early (before 8am) for a lower queue number. Bring a book or charger. Ask staff where to wait after each step.

name: Antenatal care (first visit)
documents: NHIS card, ID, any old antenatal records, partner contact if the facility requests emergency details.
bring: Urine sample in a clean container if your clinic expects it on first visit — ask locally, water, snacks.
process: Register at ANC clinic; complete intake forms.
Urine and possibly blood tests; vitals and history taken.
Consultation with midwife or doctor; dating scan may be scheduled.
Counselling on nutrition, danger signs, and next visits.
Receive next appointment date.
nhis: Routine ANC visits are often covered; some tests or supplements may require payment — confirm at the facility.
time: About 2–3 hours for first booking; allow a full morning.
tips: Call ahead to confirm if first-morning urine is needed. Wear easy clothing for examination.

name: Antenatal care (follow-up)
documents: NHIS card, antenatal record book, previous lab results if any.
bring: Urine sample if your clinic asks each visit, water, snack.
process: Check in at ANC desk with your book.
Urine dipstick and vitals (weight, BP).
Queue for midwife/doctor review.
Any scheduled immunizations or labs.
Next appointment written in your book.
nhis: Usually covered similarly to first visit; extra tests may be billed separately.
tips: Book early in the day when possible. Note any new swelling, headache, or vision changes to report.

name: Dental check-up
documents: NHIS if accepted for dental at that facility, ID, cash/mobile money for materials sometimes charged.
bring: Toothbrush if asked to clean before exam; arrive with brushed teeth when possible.
process: Register and pay if required.
Initial oral exam; X-ray may be ordered.
Treatment plan discussed (cleaning, filling, referral).
Procedure same day or new appointment.
Prescriptions to pharmacy if given.
nhis: Dental benefit on NHIS varies — many items are out-of-pocket; confirm at the dental desk.
time: About 45–90 minutes for a simple check; longer with X-ray or procedure.
tips: Morning appointments may run closer to time. Mention pain, bleeding gums, or sensitivity clearly.

name: Eye clinic
documents: NHIS if applicable, old glasses or prescription, referral if required.
bring: Sunglasses for dilation recovery; someone to accompany you if pupils will be dilated.
process: Registration and vision checks.
Tonometry or other tests as needed.
Ophthalmic consultation.
Glasses prescription or medication.
Theatre booking only if surgery planned.
nhis: Basic consultation may be covered; lenses, frames, and some drops often self-pay.
time: About 1–3 hours; longer if drops need time to work.
tips: Do not drive yourself if dilating drops are used. Bring a list of current medications.

name: Lab tests (blood, urine, etc.)
documents: Lab request form from doctor, NHIS card, hospital number, ID if requested.
bring: Fasting if your test requires it (often 8–12 hours — confirm on the form), water only if allowed, clean container for urine if take-home sample.
process: Present request at lab reception.
Payment or NHIS verification.
Sample collection (blood, urine, stool, etc.).
Ask when results will be ready and where to collect them.
Return results to your requesting doctor.
nhis: Many routine tests are covered; specialised panels may be partly or fully paid by patient.
time: Sample collection often 30–60 minutes of waiting; result turnaround varies.
tips: Arrive early for fasting slots. Label containers carefully. Keep receipt and lab number.

name: X-ray / imaging
documents: Radiology request form, NHIS card, payment receipt if required.
bring: Remove metal/jewellery as instructed; wear clothes without zippers if possible for some scans.
process: Register at radiology.
Wait for technologist call.
Positioning and exposure (follow instructions to hold still).
Collect film/report when ready or sent electronically to doctor.
nhis: Basic X-rays may be covered; CT/MRI often largely out-of-pocket unless special approval.
time: About 1–2 hours including queue; complex scans may be scheduled another day.
tips: Pregnant or possibly pregnant patients must inform staff before X-ray. Bring previous images for comparison.

name: Pharmacy only
documents: Prescription or last visit card, NHIS, payment method.
bring: Old drug packages if you need a refill with same brand; cooler bag for items needing cold chain.
process: Queue at pharmacy window with valid prescription.
NHIS verification for covered medicines.
Pay co-payment or full cost for non-covered items.
Check labels: dose, duration, and storage.
Ask how to take with food or empty stomach.
nhis: NHIS formulary covers specific drugs; brand upgrades or non-listed drugs are usually paid by patient.
time: About 20–60 minutes depending on queue and stock availability.
tips: Generic substitutes may be cheaper if NHIS does not cover a brand. Confirm expiry dates before leaving.

name: Child welfare clinic (weighing)
documents: Child health record book, NHIS, immunisation card.
bring: Diaper bag, feeding supplies, small towel; baby in light clothing for accurate weight.
process: Register at CWC.
Growth monitoring: weight, height, sometimes MUAC.
Nutrition counselling and vitamin A or deworming when due.
Immunizations if scheduled.
Next visit date recorded.
nhis: Often covered for routine CWC services; some supplements may have small charges.
tips: Arrive early. Track feeding concerns to mention.

name: Family planning
bring: List of current medicines; calendar of last period if discussing hormonal methods.
process: Private counselling on options.
Basic vitals and sometimes pregnancy test.
Method provided or referral.
Follow-up date for side effects review.
nhis: Many services subsidised or free in public facilities; confirm implant/IUCD fees locally.
time: About 45–90 minutes; implant or IUCD may need a longer slot.
tips: Ask about side effects and what to do if heavy bleeding or severe headache occurs.

name: Emergency / A&E
documents: Any ID, NHIS, contact of relative, list of allergies and medicines.
bring: Cash/mobile money for items not covered; minimal belongings so staff can work quickly.
process: Triage nurse assesses urgency — most serious cases seen first.
Registration may happen at bedside in critical cases.
Examination, tests, and treatment in emergency unit.
Admission to ward or discharge with prescription.
nhis: Emergency fees and some consumables may be billed; stabilisation is priority.
time: Highly variable: minutes for critical care; several hours for non-urgent complaints.
tips: Stay calm; give short clear history. One family spokesperson reduces confusion.

name: Specialist referral
documents: Referral letter from first doctor, NHIS, all prior results and imaging, hospital folder.
bring: Chronological list of symptoms and treatments tried; questions written down.
process: Book specialist clinic (may be weeks ahead).
Register and pay specialist levy if required.
Consultation; specialist may order new tests.
Plan: surgery, medical treatment, or further referral.
Follow-up appointment scheduling.
nhis: Specialist levies and some procedures may be partly covered — varies by hospital and scheme.
time: First visit often 2–4 hours including tests; review visits shorter.
tips: Bring copies of results for your own file. Ask when to return if symptoms worsen before the next date.
